arc_summary: This introduction is **structurally inverted**: it opens with a full M3 announcement (sentences 1–3) before stepping back to build M1 territory through biographical and introspective evidence. The **M1 block is entirely retrospective justification**, assembled after the contribution is already on record. A single embedded **M2 moment** (sentence 22) briefly concedes a measurement problem, only to be immediately absorbed into a methodological scoping claim. The paper closes by situating its associative definition within British associationist lineage — using historical precedent as warrant rather than gap.

move_roles.role: Opens the paper with a full announcement of purpose, scope, and structure — claiming the contribution before any territory has been established.
Builds retrospective justification for the contribution already announced, surveying cross-domain introspective accounts of creativity to ground the associative definition in observed patterns.
Delivers the paper's core theoretical object — the formal associative definition of creative thinking — and sharpens it by distinguishing creativity from mere originality.
Briefly acknowledges that the usefulness criterion in the definition is difficult to apply reliably across creative fields, creating a momentary niche before the paper scopes its own resolution.

Closes by delimiting the methodological scope of the current research, operationalizing originality, and situating the framework within the British associationist tradition as historical legitimation.

sentences.text: The intent of this paper is the presentation of an associative interpretation of the process of creative thinking.
sentences.move_note: M3-first opening; paper announces its purpose before establishing territory.

sentences.text: The explanation is not directed to any specific field of application such as art or science but attempts to delineate processes that underlie all creative thought.
sentences.note: Elaborates the scope of the contribution announced in [1].

sentences.text: The discussion will take the following form, (a) First, we will define creative thinking in associative terms and indicate three ways in which creative solutions may be achieved—serendipity, similarity, and mediation, (b) This definition will allow us to deduce those individual difference variables which will facilitate creative performance, (c) Consideration of the definition of the creative process has suggested an operational statement of the definition in the form of a test. The test will be briefly described along with some preliminary research results. (d) The paper will conclude with a discussion of predictions regarding the influence of certain experimentally manipulable variables upon the creative process.
sentences.note: Announces the structure and scope of the paper across four sub-goals.

sentences.text: Creative individuals and the processes by which they manifest their creativity have excited a good deal of interest and curiosity.
sentences.note: Asserts the significance and broad appeal of the topic of creativity research.
sentences.move_note: Paper steps back to M1 territory-establishment after the initial M3 opening.

sentences.text: There are extended analyses of novels and novelists, poems and poets, mathematics and mathematicians, both biographical and autobiographical.
sentences.note: Reports the existing body of work on creative individuals across domains.

sentences.text: Perusal of the introspections of manifestly creative individuals uncovers a surprising vein of similarity in the processes they describe (Ghiselin, 1952).
sentences.note: Reports a citation-backed finding — cross-domain similarity in creative introspection.

sentences.text: Thus we find Albert Einstein's self-searching to suggest that 'The psychical entities which seem to serve as elements in thought are certain signs and more or less clear images which can be combined . . . This combinatory play seems to be the essential feature in productive thought.'
sentences.note: Illustrates the cross-domain similarity in creative introspection claimed in [6].

sentences.text: Samuel Taylor Coleridge is described as having developed his ideas in the following manner: 'Facts which sank at intervals out of conscious recollection drew together beneath the surface through the almost chemical affinities of common elements.'
sentences.note: Second illustrative example of the similarity in creative introspective accounts; re-parents to [6] (tempstat), skipping [7] (secondary).

sentences.text: In the field of art, we find Andre Breton referring to a collage by Ernst as being distinguished by a 'marvelous capacity to grasp two mutually distant realities without going beyond the field of our experience and to draw a spark from the juxtaposition.'
sentences.note: Third illustrative example of the cross-domain pattern; re-parents to [6].

sentences.text: Most explicit, however, is the oft-quoted statement by the mathematician, Poincare, who talks about an evening when 'ideas rose in crowds; I felt them collide until pairs interlocked so to speak, making a stable combination. By next morning I had established the existence of a class of Fuchsian functions.'
sentences.note: Fourth illustrative example of creative introspection; re-parents to [6].

sentences.text: From these experiences, Poincare felt that he could state that 'to create consists of making new combinations of associative elements which are useful.'
sentences.note: Reports Poincaré's explicit theoretical conclusion drawn from his own experience; a named-source claim about the nature of creativity. Serves as a new primary parent for what follows.

sentences.text: The mathematical facts worthy of being studied . . . are those which reveal to us unsuspected kinships between other facts well known but wrongly believed to be strangers to one another.
sentences.note: Continues reporting Poincaré's stated theoretical position with substantive content about mathematical creativity; elevated from extension:illustrative because [11] was itself secondary in the original (now corrected to tempstat), and this sentence carries independent reportable content attributable to a named source.

sentences.text: Among chosen combinations the most fertile will often be those formed of elements drawn from domains which are far apart.
sentences.note: Continues the Poincaré quotation, further specifying the key claim; parents to [12] (tempstat).

sentences.text: An exceptionally compelling illustration of a useful combination of elements 'drawn from domains which are far apart' occurs in a line from the poem, 'The Monkey Puzzle' by Marianne Moore (1951), 'The lion's ferocious chrysanthemum head.'
sentences.note: Elevated from extension:illustrative because [13] is secondary and cannot parent a secondary sentence. The sentence independently asserts the existence and salience of a concrete case, making a primary claim about the relevance of a named example.

sentences.text: We will state our basic hypothesis regarding the nature of creative thinking in the form of a definition.
sentences.note: Announces the imminent formal definition that constitutes the paper's core theoretical move.
sentences.move_note: Return to M3 after extended M1 territory block.

sentences.text: With these introspective statements serving as background, we may proceed to define the creative thinking process as the forming of associative elements into new combinations which either meet specified requirements or are in some way useful.
sentences.note: Introduces the paper's central theoretical definition of creative thinking as a new conceptual object to be used throughout.

sentences.text: The more mutually remote the elements of the new combination, the more creative the process or solution.
sentences.note: Unpacks a key dimension of the definition introduced in [16].

sentences.text: An additional criterion of the level of creativeness of a product is described below.
sentences.note: Announces that a further criterion will be presented later in the paper; forward-scoping claim about paper content.

sentences.text: Creative thinking as defined here is distinguished from original thinking by the imposition of requirements on originality.
sentences.note: Introduces a conceptual distinction between creative and original thinking that is foundational to the paper's framework.

sentences.text: Thus, 7,363,474 is quite an original answer to the problem 'How much is 12 + 12?' However, it is only when conditions are such that this answer is useful that we can also call it creative.
sentences.note: Illustrates the distinction between originality and creativity established in [19].

sentences.text: There are many original ideas expressed in institutions for the mentally ill and mentally retarded; few of these are likely to be creative.
sentences.note: Elevated from extension:illustrative because [20] is secondary and cannot parent a secondary sentence. The sentence independently asserts a real-world pattern to reinforce the originality/creativity distinction — fits emph as a claim about a phenomenon that is real and worth attending to.

sentences.text: There are many fields of creative endeavor in which the usefulness of products would be difficult to measure reliably.
sentences.note: Acknowledges a limitation of the usefulness criterion within the proposed definition.
sentences.move_note: Brief M2 moment embedded within M3 block; acknowledges a gap/limitation in the proposed framework before the methodological scope is announced.

sentences.text: While these difficulties must eventually be faced, for the present our research efforts have been concentrated on laboratory situations in which criteria for usefulness can be arbitrarily experimenter-defined and unequivocally explained to the subject.
sentences.note: Announces the methodological scope of the current work in response to the limitation acknowledged in [22].
sentences.move_note: Return to M3 after brief M2 gap acknowledgment; M2->M3 micro-cycle.

sentences.text: The originality of a response is simply inversely related to its probability in a given population.
sentences.note: Introduces a formal operational definition of originality as a conceptual tool for the framework.

sentences.text: It should be pointed out that this definition of creativity is quite similar to basic notions advanced by British associationists from Locke (1690) to Bain (1855), and by those psychologists whose work is based in large measure on their speculations.
sentences.note: Situates the paper's definition within a historical lineage of associationist thought.
sentences.move_note: Contextualizes the contribution by relating it to prior tradition; functions as justification for the framework.

sentences.text: Freud (1938), Hollingsworth (1928), and Binet (1899) may serve as examples.
sentences.note: Names specific exemplars of the prior tradition referenced in [25]; parents to [25] (tempstat).
